title: Processes, Signals, Jobs, and Resource Control · Terminal Trace: Priority Limits
summary: Diagnose a worker that appears hung while preserving evidence, avoiding collateral termination, and distinguishing shell jobs from system processes. Planned artifact: Add and verify the linux priority limits increment in a process-tree incident and controlled-recovery runbook. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Interpret nice values, scheduling priority, resource limits, and pressure evidence without equating priority with guaranteed resources.
Use foreground, background, jobs, fg, bg, wait, disown, and nohup with terminal and session behavior in mind.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Predict Priority Limits
steps.instruction:
theory case — Process tree has shell 2100, worker 2201, and child 2207; the worker stops responding but owns a temporary report. Identify the intended process, capture state, send the least disruptive signal, and verify cleanup.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Priority Limits contract for the supplied data.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for interpret nice values, scheduling priority, resource limits, and pressure evidence without equating priority with guaranteed resources.

options.text: Interpret nice values, scheduling priority, resource limits, and pressure evidence without equating priority with guaranteed resources. Apply it to the concrete input and state the observable result.
A negative nice value means a process uses fewer resources. This does not resolve predict priority limits.

For predict priority limits, apply Priority Limits without its stated preconditions, omit the a process-tree incident and controlled-recovery runbook trace and counterexample, and treat one plausible output as complete proof. This does not satisfy Priority Limits for a process-tree incident and controlled-recovery runbook.

content.text: Interpret nice values, scheduling priority, resource limits, and pressure evidence without equating priority with guaranteed resources. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Capture identity, path, process, file, and service state; predict parsing and scope; run one safe simulated observation; interpret exit status; then change one bounded layer and verify rollback. Apply that move specifically to Priority Limits while producing a process-tree incident and controlled-recovery runbook.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Process tree has shell 2100, worker 2201, and child 2207; the worker stops responding but owns a temporary report. Identify the intended process, capture state, send the least disruptive signal, and verify cleanup. Competency probe: Interpret nice values, scheduling priority, resource limits, and pressure evidence without equating priority with guaranteed resources. A defensible solution applies Priority Limits, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a process-tree incident and controlled-recovery runbook.

content.code:
# Worked focus: linux-priority-limits — Interpret nice values, scheduling priority, resource limits, and pressure evidence without equating priority with guaranteed resources.
pgrep -a -P 2100
ps -o pid,ppid,stat,ni,cmd -p 2201
kill -TERM 2201
# Verify exit and cleanup before considering a stronger signal.
content.caption: Runnable safe terminal evidence example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: A negative nice value means a process uses fewer resources. This fails the Priority Limits contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.
